title: Correspondence: a single letter vs. a whole exchange
audience: Personal papers, institutional correspondence, email archives
intro: Correspondence forces an early modelling fork: are you describing one letter, or an exchange of many? This scenario branches on that decision, models the right entity for each path, then re-joins to attach a correspondent — so you can see how RiC handles both shapes with the same primitives.
question: Should a letter be a Record, and a back-and-forth exchange a Record Set? How do I attach the people who wrote them?

steps.prompt: What are you cataloguing here — one letter, or a whole exchange between correspondents?

choices.label: A single letter
choices.why: Good — a single letter is one unit of content: a Record. Next we'll create it.

choices.label: The whole exchange / thread
choices.why: Good — an exchange is an aggregation of letters that belong together: a Record Set. Next we'll create it, and individual letters can be added as members.

choices.label: A Record Part
choices.why: Doesn't fit either way. A letter stands on its own as a Record; an exchange is an aggregation (Record Set). A Record Part is a component of a single record — e.g. an enclosure within one letter — not the letter or the thread.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. One letter = one Record. Its date, place of writing and subjects attach as relations; an enclosure would be a Record Part of it.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Too much for one letter — a Record Set is for the exchange, not a single item.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: That's the physical sheet / scanned image. The letter's content is the Record; the carrier is an Instantiation of it.

capture.comment: Create the letter as a Record.

body.title: Letter, A. Smith to B. Jones, 1923-04-12

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Fits. The exchange is an aggregation of letters grouped because they belong together — a Record Set. Each letter is then a Record that is a member of (part of) this set.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Undersells it. One Record can't capture that the exchange is made of many distinct letters with their own dates and authors. Use a Record Set whose members are the letters.

choices.label: Activity
choices.why: Doesn't fit. The act of corresponding could be modelled as an Activity, but the letters themselves are records — the exchange is their aggregation, a Record Set.

capture.comment: Create the exchange as a Record Set. Add each letter as a member afterwards (POST /record-parts or a member relation).

steps.prompt: Now attach the writer (the person who sent it). How?

choices.label: Agent (Person) + link with has_creator
choices.why: Fits. The writer is a Person (an Agent). Create them once and relate the letter/exchange to them — here with has_creator. RiC also has more specific relations (sender/addressee); has_creator is the safe general choice and is reused across everything that person wrote.

choices.label: Type the name into the title
choices.why: Doesn't fit. A name in the title can't be reconciled or linked to the writer's other letters. Model the person as a first-class Agent.

capture.comment: Create the writer as an Agent (Person).
Link the record (the letter, or the exchange — whichever you created) to the writer with has_creator.

outcome.verdict: Same primitives, two shapes — both correct.
outcome.summary: A single letter is a Record; an exchange is a Record Set whose members are letters. Either way, the writer is a reusable Agent linked by has_creator. The fork you took at the start is exactly the judgement RiC leaves to the archivist — and both branches land on a clean, linked description.

links.label: Record Set (RiC-E03) in the model navigator
